params.text:
Dealing with confrontation safely

Care work sometimes involves confrontation, distress or behaviour that feels challenging. A person may become upset or aggressive for many reasons — pain, fear, confusion, frustration, or an unmet need they cannot express. Behaviour that seems difficult is often a form of communication.

When a situation becomes tense, your first priority is safety — yours, the person's and anyone else nearby. Helpful approaches include:

  • staying calm, speaking gently and not raising your voice
  • giving the person space and time, and not crowding them
  • listening and trying to understand what is behind the behaviour
  • removing or reducing anything that is triggering the distress, where you safely can
  • knowing when to step back and summon help rather than trying to manage alone

After a difficult situation

Never put yourself in danger. If a situation escalates beyond what you can safely manage, withdraw and call for support. Afterwards, you should always:

  • record what happened factually and objectively
  • report the incident to your manager
  • reflect on any triggers or warning signs, so the team can plan to prevent a repeat
  • seek support for yourself — difficult situations can be distressing, and it is fine to ask to talk them through

params.question: A person you support suddenly becomes angry and starts shouting. What is the safest and most respectful response?

answers.text: Stay calm, give them space, speak gently and try to understand what is behind the behaviour

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A calm, patient approach keeps everyone safer and helps you find the unmet need or trigger driving the behaviour.

answers.text: Raise your voice to show them who is in charge

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Raising your voice usually escalates the situation and can put everyone at greater risk. Staying calm is far safer.

answers.text: Physically restrain them straight away to stop the shouting

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Restraint is a last resort used only when there is a serious risk, by trained staff. It should never be your first response to shouting.

answers.text: Ignore it completely and carry on as if nothing is happening

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Ignoring distress misses the chance to understand and defuse it, and can allow the situation to escalate.
